exercises.title: GraphQL testing: GraphQlTester vs MockMvc
exercises.instructions:
Explain the difference between testing GraphQL with MockMvc vs GraphQlTester for each scenario:
A: Test that a GraphQL query returns correct data
B: Test that an unauthorized query returns an error
C: Test that a mutation creates a record in DB
D: Test a subscription that streams live price updates
E: Test that field-level validation rejects bad input
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Query returns correct data → GRAPHQLTESTER
   tester.documentName("stockQuery")
     .variable("symbol", "RELIANCE")
     .execute()
     .path("stock.price").entity(BigDecimal.class).isEqualTo(new BigDecimal("2800"))
   GraphQlTester: knows GraphQL response structure, path traversal, entity casting
   MockMvc: would need to parse JSON manually, no GraphQL-aware helpers

B: Unauthorized query → GRAPHQLTESTER
   .execute().errors().satisfy(errors ->
     assertThat(errors).anyMatch(e -> e.getErrorType() == ErrorType.UNAUTHORIZED))
   GraphQlTester: errors() builder understands GraphQL error structure
   MockMvc: would need to parse {"errors":[...]} manually

C: Mutation creates record → BOTH WORK, but GraphQlTester cleaner
   GraphQlTester: .documentName("createOrder").variable("input", input).execute()
     .path("createOrder.orderId").entity(String.class).matches(id -> id.startsWith("ORD-"))
   @DataJpaTest: separately test DB persistence (not via GraphQL layer)

D: Subscription → GRAPHQLTESTER only
   .documentName("priceSubscription")
   .executeSubscription()
   .toFlux().take(3).collectList().block()  // collect first 3 events
   MockMvc: no built-in subscription support

E: Field-level validation → GRAPHQLTESTER
   .variable("amount", -100)  // invalid
   .execute().errors().satisfy(errors ->
     assertThat(errors.get(0).getMessage()).contains("must be positive"))
   GraphQlTester: inspect error messages from GraphQL error extensions

test_cases.expected:
A: Query returns correct data → GRAPHQLTESTER
   tester.documentName("stockQuery")
     .variable("symbol", "RELIANCE")
     .execute()
     .path("stock.price").entity(BigDecimal.class).isEqualTo(new BigDecimal("2800"))
   GraphQlTester: knows GraphQL response structure, path traversal, entity casting
   MockMvc: would need to parse JSON manually, no GraphQL-aware helpers

B: Unauthorized query → GRAPHQLTESTER
   .execute().errors().satisfy(errors ->
     assertThat(errors).anyMatch(e -> e.getErrorType() == ErrorType.UNAUTHORIZED))
   GraphQlTester: errors() builder understands GraphQL error structure
   MockMvc: would need to parse {"errors":[...]} manually

C: Mutation creates record → BOTH WORK, but GraphQlTester cleaner
   GraphQlTester: .documentName("createOrder").variable("input", input).execute()
     .path("createOrder.orderId").entity(String.class).matches(id -> id.startsWith("ORD-"))
   @DataJpaTest: separately test DB persistence (not via GraphQL layer)

D: Subscription → GRAPHQLTESTER only
   .documentName("priceSubscription")
   .executeSubscription()
   .toFlux().take(3).collectList().block()  // collect first 3 events
   MockMvc: no built-in subscription support

E: Field-level validation → GRAPHQLTESTER
   .variable("amount", -100)  // invalid
   .execute().errors().satisfy(errors ->
     assertThat(errors.get(0).getMessage()).contains("must be positive"))
   GraphQlTester: inspect error messages from GraphQL error extensions

hints: GraphQlTester: knows path notation data.field.subfield
Subscription: only GraphQlTester supports .executeSubscription()
Errors: .errors().satisfy(list -> ...) for error assertions

exercises.title: Complete the @GraphQlTest setup
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks in the GraphQL test class.

blanks.hint: the GraphQL variable name matching the query argument

hints: @GraphQlTest: loads only GraphQL controllers, not full Spring context
GraphQlTester: Spring's test client for GraphQL (not RestTemplate)
.variable("symbol", value): passes named variable to the query

exercises.title: Simple GraphQL query test
exercises.instructions:
Write a test for a Zerodha stock price query:
1. @GraphQlTest(StockController.class)
2. @MockBean StockService
3. documentName('stockBySymbol') with variable symbol='TCS'
4. Assert: path('stockBySymbol.price') equals 3500
5. Include the .graphql test document content
hints: documentName: maps to src/test/resources/graphql-test/*.graphql
.path('stockBySymbol.price'): dot-notation traversal of response
.entity(BigDecimal.class).isEqualTo(): type-safe assertion

exercises.title: Test GraphQL mutation
exercises.instructions:
Test a Razorpay payment creation mutation:
1. @GraphQlTest with mock PaymentService
2. documentName('createPayment') with input variables
3. Assert: path('createPayment.paymentId') starts with 'PAY-'
4. Assert: path('createPayment.status') equals 'INITIATED'
5. Include mutation .graphql file content
hints: .errors().verify(): asserts zero errors (call before .path() assertions)
.errors().satisfy(): inspect errors when errors ARE expected
Map.of(): pass complex input types as maps (GraphQL serializes automatically)

exercises.title: Test GraphQL errors and error types
exercises.instructions:
Write tests specifically for GraphQL error scenarios:
1. NOT_FOUND: query for non-existent stock
2. BAD_REQUEST: mutation with invalid amount
3. UNAUTHORIZED: query without auth token
4. Error type assertion using GraphQLError.getErrorType()
5. Error message content assertion
hints: .errors().satisfy(): inspect the error list — size, type, message
.document(inlineGql): use inline query string instead of file
ErrorType: enum in spring-graphql (NOT_FOUND, BAD_REQUEST, UNAUTHORIZED, etc.)

exercises.title: Test GraphQL list responses
exercises.instructions:
Test a GraphQL query returning a list of stocks:
1. mockStocks: list of 3 Stock objects
2. Query: allStocks (no args)
3. Assert list size == 3
4. Assert first element symbol
5. Assert list is not empty — entityList() pattern
hints: .entityList(Type.class): deserialize the response array into typed list
.hasSize(N): assert exact count
.path('allStocks[0].symbol'): access list element by index via path notation

exercises.title: Test GraphQL subscription
exercises.instructions:
Test a Zerodha live price subscription:
1. documentName('livePriceSubscription') for symbol='RELIANCE'
2. executeSubscription() returns Flux
3. Collect first 3 price updates
4. Assert: prices are positive, in ascending order
5. @GraphQlTest
hints: .executeSubscription().toFlux(): convert to Flux<ResponseSpec>
.take(3): only collect first 3 events (stop infinite stream)
.block(Duration.ofSeconds(5)): wait max 5s for collection

exercises.title: Test DataLoader batch loading
exercises.instructions:
Test that DataLoader is invoked (not N+1 queries):
1. Mock TradeRepository to capture batch calls
2. GraphQL query: portfolio with 3 positions, each needing trade history
3. Verify: repository called ONCE with batch of 3 (not 3 separate calls)
4. DataLoader test: assert batchLoadFn invoked with all keys at once
hints: verify(repo, atMost(1)): assert batch repository called at most once (not N times)
argThat(list -> list.containsAll(...)): verify all symbols in one batch call
DataLoader batching is the key N+1 prevention mechanism

exercises.title: Full integration test with HttpGraphQlTester
exercises.instructions:
Write integration tests using HttpGraphQlTester (hits real HTTP endpoint):
1. @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment=RANDOM_PORT)
2. HttpGraphQlTester.create(WebTestClient)
3. Query real endpoint (no mocks)
4. Verify data comes from @MockBean service
5. Test includes HTTP header (authorization)
hints: HttpGraphQlTester.create(WebTestClient): wraps WebTestClient with GraphQL support
RANDOM_PORT: starts real server on random port (avoids port conflicts)
defaultHeader: add auth header to all requests in this tester

exercises.title: Analyze failing GraphQL test
exercises.expected_solution:
IDENTIFIED ISSUE: Missing .variable() call

ROOT CAUSE:
  The GraphQL query 'getPortfolio' requires an $accountId variable:
  query GetPortfolio($accountId: String!) {
    portfolio(accountId: $accountId) { totalValue }
  }
  The test calls .execute() without providing the required variable.
  GraphQL rejects the query: 'accountId is required but not provided'

FIX:
  tester.documentName("getPortfolio")
    .variable("accountId", "ZER-001")  // ← ADD THIS
    .execute()
    .path("portfolio.totalValue")
    .entity(BigDecimal.class)
    .isEqualTo(new BigDecimal("150000"));

ADDITIONAL ISSUE: Missing .errors().verify()
  Without .errors().verify(), GraphQL errors are silently ignored.
  If the resolver throws an exception, the response might have partial data
  AND errors — the test would pass even if there were errors!
  Always add .errors().verify() before .path() assertions.

CORRECT FIX:
  tester.documentName("getPortfolio")
    .variable("accountId", "ZER-001")
    .execute()
    .errors().verify()  // ← ADD THIS TOO
    .path("portfolio.totalValue")
    .entity(BigDecimal.class)
    .isEqualTo(new BigDecimal("150000"));

SECOND HIDDEN ISSUE: Mock argument mismatch
  when(portfolioService.getPortfolio("ZER-001")) — this is fine if variable matches.
  But if variable name in test is 'accountId' and mock expects specific value: test passes.
  If you later change the variable to 'ZER-002' but forget to update mock: mock returns null → NPE.
  Better: when(portfolioService.getPortfolio(anyString())).thenReturn(...)

test_cases.expected:
IDENTIFIED ISSUE: Missing .variable() call

ROOT CAUSE:
  The GraphQL query 'getPortfolio' requires an $accountId variable:
  query GetPortfolio($accountId: String!) {
    portfolio(accountId: $accountId) { totalValue }
  }
  The test calls .execute() without providing the required variable.
  GraphQL rejects the query: 'accountId is required but not provided'

FIX:
  tester.documentName("getPortfolio")
    .variable("accountId", "ZER-001")  // ← ADD THIS
    .execute()
    .path("portfolio.totalValue")
    .entity(BigDecimal.class)
    .isEqualTo(new BigDecimal("150000"));

ADDITIONAL ISSUE: Missing .errors().verify()
  Without .errors().verify(), GraphQL errors are silently ignored.
  If the resolver throws an exception, the response might have partial data
  AND errors — the test would pass even if there were errors!
  Always add .errors().verify() before .path() assertions.

CORRECT FIX:
  tester.documentName("getPortfolio")
    .variable("accountId", "ZER-001")
    .execute()
    .errors().verify()  // ← ADD THIS TOO
    .path("portfolio.totalValue")
    .entity(BigDecimal.class)
    .isEqualTo(new BigDecimal("150000"));

SECOND HIDDEN ISSUE: Mock argument mismatch
  when(portfolioService.getPortfolio("ZER-001")) — this is fine if variable matches.
  But if variable name in test is 'accountId' and mock expects specific value: test passes.
  If you later change the variable to 'ZER-002' but forget to update mock: mock returns null → NPE.
  Better: when(portfolioService.getPortfolio(anyString())).thenReturn(...)

hints: .variable() missing: required query variables must be provided
.errors().verify(): always add before .path() — otherwise errors silently ignored
anyString(): more resilient mock matcher than exact string

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha GraphQL test suite
exercises.instructions:
Build complete test suite for Zerodha GraphQL API:
1. stockBySymbol query test
2. createOrder mutation test
3. Error test: NOT_FOUND for unknown stock
4. Subscription test: livePriceUpdates
5. @GraphQlTest (4 tests): all pass
hints: .errors().verify() before .path(): always check no errors first
.take(2) on subscription Flux: limit how many events to collect
.mapNotNull(r -> r.toEntity(Type.class)): deserialize each subscription event

exercises.title: Test GraphQL security with unauthorized access
exercises.instructions:
Test GraphQL authorization:
1. Query without auth token → UNAUTHORIZED error
2. Query with valid token → data returned
3. @WithMockUser for authenticated tests
4. Verify error type is UNAUTHORIZED (not INTERNAL_ERROR)
5. @GraphQlTest with security config
hints: @WithMockUser: sets Spring Security context for the test method
Anonymous = no annotation: simulates unauthenticated request
Public vs protected: GraphQL queries can have different security requirements per field

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay GraphQL integration test
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay payment GraphQL integration test:
1. @SpringBootTest(RANDOM_PORT) with HttpGraphQlTester
2. Query: getAllPayments (mock list of 5)
3. Mutation: initiatePayment (returns paymentId)
4. Error: initiatePayment with zero amount → error
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): list, mutation, validation error
hints: HttpGraphQlTester: starts real HTTP server (WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT)
defaultHeader: API key sent with every test request
eq(0L): exact value matcher — only matches zero amount

exercises.title: GraphQL performance test — response time assertions
exercises.instructions:
Write GraphQL performance tests:
1. Measure query execution time
2. Assert complex query (portfolio with positions) < 200ms
3. Assert simple query (stock price) < 50ms
4. Detect N+1: measure with 1 position vs 100 positions (should be similar time)
hints: Mocked tests: timing not realistic for real perf (mocks are instant)
N+1 detection: compare time for 1 item vs 100 items — similar = good batching
Real perf testing: use Gatling/k6 against running server (M25 T4 patterns)

exercises.title: Complete M27 T5 GraphQL Testing synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M27 T5 synthesis:
1. @GraphQlTest with mock StockService
2. Query test (symbol → stock data)
3. Mutation test (createOrder → orderId)
4. Error test (unknown stock → error list non-empty)
5. Subscription test (collect 2 price updates)
6. @GraphQlTest (4 tests): all pass
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M27 T5 GraphQL Testing synthesis
hints: errors().verify() before path(): always check no errors first
subscription take(2): limit collection from infinite stream
isEqualByComparingTo: BigDecimal comparison ignoring scale
